summary: Fowler briefly critiques an anti-pattern where user stories are written in isolation by product owners or analysts and handed off to developers as mandates. He argues this approach fundamentally misunderstands agile principles, which emphasize collaboration over documentation handoffs. He advocates instead for conversational stories where requirements emerge through dialogue between all parties.
key_points: Stories written by product owners and 'passed to developers' represents a command-and-control mindset incompatible with agile
This approach treats developers as implementers rather than collaborative problem-solvers
Conversational stories are the preferred alternative, emphasizing ongoing dialogue over upfront specification
money_quotes: I see this as a profound misunderstanding of agile thinking
key_insight: User stories should emerge from conversation between product owners and developers, not be decreed from on high.
